description:
You are acting as a DevOps engineer who has just been paged because
    an application is failing to pick up the right environment variables.
    Your job is to prepare a clean, reproducible environment-variable setup,
    verify that those variables are correctly loaded, and capture the
    verification evidence in a log file that the automated grader will read.

    Current project structure:
        /home/user/app/
            ├── .env.sample      (already exists – see its contents below)
            └── README.md        (irrelevant to this task)

    1.  Copy the provided “/home/user/app/.env.sample” to
        “/home/user/app/.env” and replace **every** placeholder value
        in the new file with the concrete production values shown below
        (make sure there are *no* surrounding quotes or extra spaces):

            API_KEY=12345-ABCDE
            APP_ENV=production
            LOG_LEVEL=debug
            SERVICE_PORT=8080

        After your edits, the file “/home/user/app/.env” must contain
        exactly four non-empty lines, each in KEY=value format, in the
        alphabetical order shown above and terminated with a single
        trailing newline.

    2.  Export these variables into your current shell session so that
        commands such as `echo "$APP_ENV"` immediately return “production”.

    3.  Create a new directory “/home/user/app/logs” (permissions 755)
        and inside it create the file “/home/user/app/logs/env_debug.log”
        whose contents must **exactly** follow this template
        (including capitalization, blanks, line breaks,
        and the terminating newline):

            === ENV DEBUG LOG ===
            TIMESTAMP:PLACEHOLDER

            API_KEY=12345-ABCDE
            APP_ENV=production
            LOG_LEVEL=debug
            SERVICE_PORT=8080
            END-OF-LOG

        Notes on the required format:
            • The first three lines are: title line, timestamp line,
              then a completely blank line.
            • An empty line must exist between the timestamp line
              and the first KEY=value entry.
            • The KEY=value lines must appear in strict
              alphabetical order by KEY.
            • “END-OF-LOG” must be the final line
              and the file must end with a single newline.
            • Do **not** substitute a real timestamp;
              literally write the string “TIMESTAMP:PLACEHOLDER”.

    4.  When everything is ready, verify interactively that
        the variable values are available in your shell
        (for example by running `printenv | grep APP_ENV`)
        and that “/home/user/app/logs/env_debug.log”
        matches the exact required format.

    Automated grading will confirm:
        • The exact content of “/home/user/app/.env”.
        • Existence of directory “/home/user/app/logs” with correct
          permissions.
        • The exact byte-for-byte content of
          “/home/user/app/logs/env_debug.log”.

    Do not delete or modify any other files.  
    You may use any standard CLI tools available in a typical Debian/Ubuntu
    environment (e.g., cp, sed, echo, printf, mkdir, cat, etc.).
truth:
Expected final files and their full contents
    (the checks are byte-for-byte, including final newlines).

    1.  /home/user/app/.env
        ---------------------------------
        API_KEY=12345-ABCDE
        APP_ENV=production
        LOG_LEVEL=debug
        SERVICE_PORT=8080
        ⏎                                       ← single trailing newline
        ---------------------------------

    2.  /home/user/app/logs/env_debug.log
        ---------------------------------
        === ENV DEBUG LOG ===
        TIMESTAMP:PLACEHOLDER

        API_KEY=12345-ABCDE
        APP_ENV=production
        LOG_LEVEL=debug
        SERVICE_PORT=8080
        END-OF-LOG
        ⏎                                       ← single trailing newline
        ---------------------------------

    3.  Directory /home/user/app/logs must exist with mode 0755
        (u=rwx, g=rx, o=rx).

    In-memory shell environment (not file-based) is **not** directly
    tested; only the files/directories above are verified.
